question: Which country legend was known as 'The Man in Black'?
funFact: Johnny Cash explained his signature look in the 1971 song 'Man in Black,' saying he wore black on behalf of the poor, the imprisoned, and the downtrodden.

question: Which Dolly Parton song later became a massive pop hit for Whitney Houston?
funFact: Elvis Presley wanted to record 'I Will Always Love You,' but Dolly Parton refused when Colonel Tom Parker demanded half the publishing rights — a decision that later earned her millions.

question: Which artist launched her career with the 2006 debut single 'Tim McGraw'?
funFact: At age 14, Taylor Swift became the youngest songwriter ever signed by the Sony/ATV publishing house, two years before her debut single was released.

question: 'Friends in Low Places' is a signature song of which country superstar?
funFact: According to the RIAA, Garth Brooks is the best-selling solo albums artist in United States history, ahead of even Elvis Presley.

question: The Grand Ole Opry is located in which city?
funFact: The Opry began in 1925 as the WSM Barn Dance radio show, making it the longest-running radio broadcast in American history.

question: Carrie Underwood rose to fame in 2005 after winning which television competition?
funFact: After her audition, Simon Cowell predicted Underwood would not only win American Idol but outsell every previous winner — and she did.

question: Which country legend wrote and recorded 'On the Road Again'?
funFact: Willie Nelson scribbled 'On the Road Again' mid-flight on an airplane sick bag when asked to write a theme for the 1980 film Honeysuckle Rose.

question: Who recorded the 1968 country anthem 'Stand By Your Man'?
funFact: Tammy Wynette and producer Billy Sherrill wrote 'Stand By Your Man' in roughly 15 minutes at the end of a recording session.

question: Which country star is affectionately nicknamed 'King George'?
funFact: George Strait has racked up 60 number-one singles across all charts, more than any other artist in any genre of music.

question: Who had the timeless 1961 hit 'Crazy'?
funFact: Patsy Cline recorded her final 'Crazy' vocal in a single take just weeks after a near-fatal car crash, having initially struggled to hit the notes because of broken ribs.

question: Which group recorded the 2009 crossover smash 'Need You Now'?
funFact: 'Need You Now' won four Grammy Awards in 2011, including both Record of the Year and Song of the Year, beating out pop heavyweights.

question: Which Canadian-born star released 'Man! I Feel Like a Woman!'?
funFact: Shania Twain's 1997 album Come On Over is the best-selling country album of all time and the best-selling studio album ever by a solo female artist.

question: Which country singer served as a coach on The Voice for its first 23 seasons?
funFact: Blake Shelton won The Voice a record nine times as a coach — and met his wife, Gwen Stefani, when she joined the show as a fellow coach.

question: Who had the 1992 line-dance phenomenon 'Achy Breaky Heart'?
funFact: Twenty-seven years after 'Achy Breaky Heart,' Billy Ray Cyrus returned to the top of the charts on Lil Nas X's record-breaking 'Old Town Road' remix.

question: Who sang the 1978 storytelling classic 'The Gambler'?
funFact: 'The Gambler' was written by Don Schlitz and recorded by several artists first, but Kenny Rogers' version won a Grammy and spawned a series of TV movies starring Rogers himself.

question: Whose version of 'Wagon Wheel' hit number one on the country charts in 2013?
funFact: 'Wagon Wheel' began as an unfinished Bob Dylan sketch from 1973; Ketch Secor of Old Crow Medicine Show wrote verses around Dylan's chorus decades later.

question: Which instrument does the devil play in Charlie Daniels' 'The Devil Went Down to Georgia'?
funFact: The song won the Charlie Daniels Band a Grammy in 1979, and the devil's frantic fiddle solo was built by layering multiple overdubbed fiddle tracks.

question: Tim McGraw is married to which fellow country star?
funFact: Married in 1996, McGraw and Hill launched the Soul2Soul II Tour in 2006, which became one of the highest-grossing tours in country music history.

question: Who recorded the beloved 1971 anthem 'Take Me Home, Country Roads'?
funFact: Co-written with Bill Danoff and Taffy Nivert, the song was officially adopted as a state song of West Virginia in 2014.

question: Which artist had the 2017 hit 'Body Like a Back Road'?
funFact: 'Body Like a Back Road' spent a then-record 34 weeks at number one on Billboard's Hot Country Songs chart. Sam Hunt played college football as a quarterback before turning to music.

question: Which then-struggling songwriter wrote Patsy Cline's hit 'Crazy'?
funFact: In the same prolific stretch, Willie Nelson also wrote 'Hello Walls' for Faron Young and 'Funny How Time Slips Away' — all before anyone knew him as a singer.

question: Hank Williams died on New Year's Day of which year?
funFact: Williams was only 29 when he died in the back seat of his Cadillac en route to a show in Canton, Ohio — and his single 'I'll Never Get Out of This World Alive' was on the charts at the time.

question: Which artist had a controversial 1969 hit with 'Okie From Muskogee'?
funFact: Merle Haggard was actually an inmate at San Quentin when Johnny Cash performed there on New Year's Day 1959 — an experience Haggard credited with inspiring his music career.

question: The twangy, Telecaster-driven 'Bakersfield Sound' of the 1960s came out of which U.S. state?
funFact: Pioneered by Buck Owens and Merle Haggard, the Bakersfield Sound arose as a raw honky-tonk alternative to the string-laden polish of the 'Nashville Sound.'

question: Which singer wrote and recorded the autobiographical hit 'Coal Miner's Daughter'?
funFact: The 1980 film adaptation of Coal Miner's Daughter earned Sissy Spacek the Academy Award for Best Actress for her portrayal of Loretta Lynn — Spacek did all her own singing.

question: In 1999, Garth Brooks released a pop-rock album under which fictional alter ego?
funFact: The Chris Gaines project was meant as a 'pre-soundtrack' for a movie called The Lamb that was never made, yet it gave Brooks his only Billboard Hot 100 top-five hit, 'Lost in You.'

question: Who won the very first CMA Entertainer of the Year award in 1967?
funFact: Eddy Arnold, known as 'the Tennessee Plowboy,' sold more than 85 million records and helped pioneer the smooth, crossover-friendly Nashville Sound.

question: Which group recorded the darkly comic 2000 revenge hit 'Goodbye Earl'?
funFact: 'Goodbye Earl' was written by Dennis Linde, the same songwriter behind Elvis Presley's 1972 hit 'Burning Love.'

question: Alan Jackson's 2003 chart-topper 'It's Five O'Clock Somewhere' was a duet with which singer?
funFact: The breezy duet spent eight weeks at number one on the country chart and won the CMA Vocal Event of the Year award in 2003.

question: Which country artist's album Golden Hour won the Grammy for Album of the Year in 2019?
funFact: Before her Grammy sweep, Kacey Musgraves got her television start on the singing competition Nashville Star in 2007, where she finished seventh.

question: 'He Stopped Loving Her Today,' often called the greatest country song ever recorded, was a 1980 hit for whom?
funFact: George Jones bet producer Billy Sherrill that 'nobody'll buy that morbid son of a gun' — instead it revived his career and became his signature song.

question: What 1976 compilation featuring Waylon Jennings and Willie Nelson became the first country album certified platinum?
funFact: The album also featured Jessi Colter, Waylon Jennings' wife, and Tompall Glaser, cementing the 'outlaw country' movement as a commercial force.

question: Dolly Parton's theme park Dollywood is located in which Tennessee town?
funFact: Dolly grew up nearby in Sevier County and opened Dollywood in 1986; it is now Tennessee's most-visited ticketed attraction.

question: Which duo had the 1992 honky-tonk dance hit 'Boot Scootin' Boogie'?
funFact: Brooks & Dunn won the CMA Vocal Duo of the Year award 14 times, more than any other duo in the history of the awards.

question: Chris Stapleton's breakout 2015 CMA Awards performance of 'Tennessee Whiskey' paired him with which pop star?
funFact: 'Tennessee Whiskey' was originally recorded by David Allan Coe in 1981 and made famous by George Jones in 1983 before Stapleton's soulful reinvention.

question: Reba McEntire grew up on a cattle ranch in which state?
funFact: Reba was discovered after singing the national anthem at the 1974 National Finals Rodeo in Oklahoma City; her father and grandfather were both champion steer ropers.

question: In Marty Robbins' 1959 gunfighter ballad 'El Paso,' what is the name of the Mexican maiden the cowboy loves?
funFact: Robbins kept returning to the story for decades, writing the sequel 'Feleena (From El Paso)' in 1966 and the Grammy-nominated 'El Paso City' in 1976.

question: Which singer, the youngest sister of Loretta Lynn, had the 1977 hit 'Don't It Make My Brown Eyes Blue'?
funFact: Crystal Gayle, famous for her floor-length hair, won the 1978 Grammy for Best Female Country Vocal Performance for the song, which crossed over to the pop top five.

question: Keith Urban was born in which country?
funFact: Though born in Whangarei, New Zealand, Urban was raised in Queensland, Australia, and moved to Nashville in the early 1990s years before his American breakthrough.

question: Johnny Cash's 'Ring of Fire' was co-written by Merle Kilgore and which member of the Carter Family?
funFact: June's sister Anita Carter actually recorded the song first in 1962; Johnny Cash then cut his version with mariachi-style horns he claimed came to him in a dream.

question: Which radio announcer, known as 'The Solemn Old Judge,' coined the name 'Grand Ole Opry' on the air in 1927?
funFact: Hay improvised the name right after NBC's grand opera program, quipping that for the next hour listeners would hear the 'Grand Ole Opry' instead.

question: Whose 1924 recording of 'Wreck of the Old 97' backed with 'The Prisoner's Song' is considered country music's first million-seller?
funFact: Vernon Dalhart was a Texas-born light-opera tenor who recorded under more than 100 different pseudonyms during his prolific career.

question: Which producer oversaw the famous 1927 Bristol Sessions, often called the 'Big Bang of country music'?
funFact: Within days of each other at the Bristol Sessions, Ralph Peer discovered and recorded both Jimmie Rodgers and the Carter Family, launching two foundational country careers.

question: Who was the first solo female artist inducted into the Country Music Hall of Fame, in 1973?
funFact: Cline's induction came a decade after the 1963 plane crash near Camden, Tennessee, that killed her along with fellow stars Cowboy Copas and Hawkshaw Hawkins.

question: The 2000 lament 'Murder on Music Row,' decrying pop influence on country music, was a duet between Alan Jackson and whom?
funFact: Despite never being released as an official single, the song charted from radio airplay alone and won the CMA Vocal Event of the Year award in 2000.

question: Which Nashville Sound architect built the famous Quonset Hut studio, widely regarded as Music Row's first major recording studio?
funFact: Owen Bradley, who built the studio with his guitarist brother Harold in the mid-1950s, went on to produce defining records for Patsy Cline, Loretta Lynn, and Brenda Lee.

question: Who was the Grand Ole Opry's first African American star, famed for the harmonica showpiece 'Pan American Blues'?
funFact: It was DeFord Bailey's performance of 'Pan American Blues' that immediately preceded George D. Hay's on-air coining of the name 'Grand Ole Opry' in 1927; Bailey was inducted into the Country Music Hall of Fame in 2005.

question: How old was Tanya Tucker when her hit 'Delta Dawn' reached the country top ten in 1972?
funFact: Helen Reddy's pop version of 'Delta Dawn' hit number one a year later, but the teenage Tucker recorded it first, launching one of country's longest careers.

question: Willie Nelson's 1975 concept album Red Headed Stranger produced which song, his first number-one hit as a singer?
choices: Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys
Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain
funFact: 'Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain' was written by Fred Rose back in 1945 and recorded by Roy Acuff decades before Nelson's stripped-down version won him his first Grammy.

question: Which Rhodes Scholar turned songwriter wrote 'Sunday Mornin' Comin' Down,' 'Me and Bobby McGee,' and 'Help Me Make It Through the Night'?
funFact: While working as a National Guard helicopter pilot, Kristofferson famously landed a helicopter on Johnny Cash's lawn to get his song demos heard.
